coredata.dc:title: Yellow emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zihao
Chen, Da
Gu, Bingli
Gao, Bo
Liu, Zhiduo
Yang, Yongsheng
Guo, Qinglei
Zheng, Xiaohu
Wang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and sensitive fluorescence nanomaterials sensor with satisfactory selectivity has gained numerous importance for detecting multiple metal ions. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) has attracted a great deal of attentions. Herein, yellow emissive GQDs with a high quantum yield of 0.34 were achieved by nitrogen-doping. The as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior and high optical stability. Furthermore, based on the complexation between Fe3+ and N-GQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs could be greatly quenched by the addition of a small amount of Fe3+ ions. The linearity range is 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 63 nM. For this reason, the proposed method was demonstrated to be selective and suitable for Fe3+ analysis in natural water samples. And, N-GQDs with the biosafety have the potentials for intracellular Fe3+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Fe3+ sensing
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-GQDs and its sensing performance for the detection of Fe3+.

Characterizations of N-GQDs. (a) Plan-view TEM image of the as-prepared N-GQDs. The inset corresponds to the size distribution histogram of N-GQDs. (b) HR-TEM image of a typical N-GQD. (c) FFT analysis pattern of N-GQDs. (d) Raman spectrum of N-GQDs on the SiO2/Si substrate. (e) Contact-mode AFM image of the N-GQDs transferred on SiO2/Si substrate. (f–g) The corresponding thicknesses of N-GQDs. (h) The height distribution histogram of N-GQDs.

Chemical structure analysis of N-GQDs. (a) Full-scan XPS spectrum of the N-GQDs. (b) C-1s XPS spectrum. (c) N-1s XPS spectrum. (d) O-1s XPS spectrum. (e) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. (f) Summary of φ and emission wavelengths of N-GQDs obtained in this work and previous reports.

Optical properties of N-GQDs. (a) UV–vis, PL and PLE spectra of N-GQDs. Photograph of N-GQDs aqueous solution under the irradiation of (b) visible light and (c) 365 nm UV light. (d) PL spectra of N-GQDs recorded with various excitation wavelengths with an increments of 20 nm (360–480 nm). PL intensity variations of the N-GQDs as a function of (e) illumination time, (f) cultured time, and (g) pH value (λex = 420 nm).

Biosafety evaluation of N-GQDs. (a) Influence of different ions on the PL intensity of N-GQDs. F and F0 are the PL intensity of N-GQDs with and without ions, respectively. (b) PL intensity of N-GQDs as a function of the ionic concentration. (c) Metabolic activity of fibroblast cells treated with N-GQDs with different concentrations. (d) Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of fibroblast cells incubated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs (λex = 420 nm).

Application exploration of N-GQDs. (a) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs at various concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 300 μM. The excitation wavelength is fixed at 420 nm. (b) The images of the change in fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs solution in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ under the excitation of sunlight and UV light. (c) The change in fluorescence intensity as a function of Fe3+ concentration. (d) Linear relationship between fluorescence and Fe3+ concentration at 0–80 μM (inset is the low level spectra).

Mechanism for Fe3+ sensing. (a) Relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+, which is fitted by a typical Stern-Volmer equation. (b) Fluorescence decay traces of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, and the addition of Fe3+ are 10 and 20 μM. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, and the addition of Fe3+ are 10 and 20 μM. (d) The zeta potentials of N-GQDs toward different Fe3+ concentrations in the system.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.
Results for the determination of Fe3+ of different water sources.

head.ce:title: Yellow emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and sensitive fluorescence nanomaterials sensor with satisfactory selectivity has gained numerous importance for detecting multiple metal ions. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) has attracted a great deal of attentions. Herein, yellow emissive GQDs with a high quantum yield of 0.34 were achieved by nitrogen-doping. The as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior and high optical stability. Furthermore, based on the complexation between Fe3+ and N-GQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs could be greatly quenched by the addition of a small amount of Fe3+ ions. The linearity range is 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 63 nM. For this reason, the proposed method was demonstrated to be selective and suitable for Fe3+ analysis in natural water samples. And, N-GQDs with the biosafety have the potentials for intracellular Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD), one of the newly developed members of graphene family, has attracted a great deal of attention owning to the excellent superiority in optical, thermal, and electronic performance [1–3]. Both pure GQDs and doped or functionalized GQDs have developed as the potential fluorescent materials in many fields, including fluorescent sensing [4], fluorescent bio-imaging [5], photovoltaic conversion [6], photon detection [7], and photocatalysis [8]. Moreover, distinguishing from traditional heavy metal QDs and fluorescent dyes, GQDs possess outstanding stability [9], low toxicity [10] and favorable biocompatibility [11]. Producing GQDs with high quality is a hot topic in these years. The existing approaches could be classified into two types: top-down and bottom-up. The former mainly focus on the exfoliation and cutting of various sp2 carbon materials of large sizes (e.g. graphene, C60, graphene oxide, carbon fiber) into GQDs which depends on the oxidation effect of concentrated acid, UV-Fenton, microwave, etc. [12–15]. The using of strong acid and oxidant in the top-down strategy leads to its procedure with harsh conditions and low yield. On the other side, the bottom-up approach transforms the small molecules precursors to integrate GQDs through dehydration and dehydrogenation. However, GQDs produced by above methods typically exhibit extremely poor optical properties. For example, their emission bands are merely within the blue region, even though their sizes increase to a few dozens of nanometers. Furthermore, their fluorescent quantum yields (QY) are rather low (<17% in most cases), which inevitably limits their extensive applications [16,17]. Thus, it is of great necessity to develop an effective method to produce high-quality GQDs with high QY.

The atom radius of nitrogen is comparable to carbon and five valence electrons of N can strengthen nitrogen-related chemical bonds to make GQDs more stable, N doping has been demonstrated to effectively improve the QY of GQDs [18]. More importantly, N atoms in graphene structures can manipulate the partial electrochemical properties, endow GQDs chemical reactivity and multiple functionalities [19,20]. Consequently, a large number of studies toward nitrogen-doped GQDs had been reported. Zhang et al. synthesized N-GQDs using hydrothermal method by utilizing marigold, and a strong blue emission for Fe3+ ions detection was demonstrated [21]. Lin et al. reported the synthesis of blue-emissive N-GQDs from citric acid and tri (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane for the detection of trinitrophenol (TNP) with a low limit [19]. Kashani et al. revealed that N-GQDs become an emerging biocompatible and label-free fluorescence probe for cellular imaging of human ACHN cell, even at high concentrations, strong and stable green fluorescence can be emitted [22]. Most of the reported GQDs display short wavelength emission from blue to green, the long wavelength (e.g. yellow light) emission is rarely achieved.

In this work, a facile and highly productive strategy is demonstrated for hydrothermal synthesis of yellow emissive N-GQDs by using dopamine (DA) as precursor. Fig. 1 shows the synthetic process of N-GQDs and its sensing performance for the detection of Fe3+. The as-prepared N-GQDs are with a size of 3.4 nm and a high QY of 0.34. It shows a strong yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Using the N-GQDs, a Fe3+ ion sensor with superior sensitivity and selectivity was designed. If natural water with Fe3+ ions was added into N-GQDs solution, the catechol moiety will be oxidized, and subsequently leading to the fluorescence quench of N-GQDs. In addition, the N doping (edged amino groups and internal lattice doping) can establish electrostatic interaction between the N-GQDs and Fe3+, which acts as another significant part in detection of Fe3+. The sensitivity of the sensing probe is manifested in a detection limit of 63 nM. And, our results demonstrate the N-GQDs based sensor is of great value in fluorescent bio-imaging in vitro.

Dopamine hydrochloride and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Solution of Fe3+ was prepared from FeCl3. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The deionized (DI) water used throughout all experiments was purified using a Millipore system. 0.38 g (2 mmol) dopamine hydrochloride and 25 mL DI water were mixed and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After being heated at 180 °C for 24 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. Then the obtained mixture was filtered by 0.22 μm Nylon filter membrane and kept for dialyzed against Millipore 18.2 MΩ·cm water through a dialysis membrane (100–500 Da MWCO) for 3 days. After that, the N-GQDs were collected by removing the large particles through centrifugation at 9000 rpm/min for 15 min and the faint yellow supernatant was freeze-dried to yield a slightly yellow powder (N-GQDs). Finally, the frozen substance was configured to the solution with water and stored at 4 °C for following use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on Hitachi H-8100. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Cypher S, Asylum Research) was used to determine the height profiles of N-GQDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) was used to determine the structure of N-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera II system, Ulvac-PHI Inc., Japan) was utilized to determine the surface chemical composition and bonding states of N-GQDs. Thermogravimetry (TGA) was performed with a Pyres 1 TGA apparatus at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 50 to 800 °C under a N2 atmosphere to investigate the thermal decomposition and thermal stabilities of N-GQDs. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption properties were characterized on UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected on a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. PL lifetime was measured via the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique (Hydra Harp 400, Pico Quant). Raman scattering (SENTERRA) with a wavelength of 532 nm was used to determine the quality. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured using a Nicolet 670 spectrograph.

The φ measurements: quinine sulfate (φ = 0.54 at 360 nm) was chosen as comparison. The QY can be calculated according to the following equation: [23].
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated PL intensity, A is the optical density, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscript R refers to the quinine sulfate.

The detection of Fe3+ by utilizing prepared N-GQDs was carried out in 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS pH = 7). In a typical strategy, 10 μL Fe3+ with various concentrations were separately added into the mixture of 200 μL of N-GQDs dispersion (5 mg/mL) and 2 mL of PBS. The resulted solutions were shaken well and incubated for 1 min before recording the fluorescence intensity under excitation at 420 nm. In addition, the selectivity for Fe3+ was demonstrated by replacing Fe3+ with other metal ion and the same steps were repeated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cells were cultured in 96-well plates with various concentrations of N-GQDs, and then incubated for 48 h. The fluorescence imaging of fibroblast cells treated with GQDs was conducted at 420 nm (Zeiss LSM-510 Meta Confocal Microscope). Afterwards, the cells were washed three times with PBS and then fixed in 1% paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Finally, the fibroblast cells were imaged using the Zeiss LSM-510 Meta confocal microscope.

Fig. 2(a) shows a typical TEM image of the as-synthesized N-GQDs. Homogeneous N-GQDs are observed with an average diameter of about 3.4 nm according to the size distribution histogram (Fig. 2(a) inset, obtained by counting about 100 particles). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image shown in Fig. 2(b) reveals a 0.24 nm lattice spacing in crystalline structure, which is assigned as the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [24]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image shows a standard six-fold symmetry as displayed in Fig. 2(c). This demonstrated the good crystalline quality of N-GQDs [19]. The XRD pattern of the N-GQDs (Fig. S1) exhibits a weak dispersed diffraction peak centered at around 2θ = 27.3° corresponds to the graphitic structure [25]. Raman spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) shows the defect related peak (D) and graphitic peak (G) locate at about 1377 and 1576 cm−1 which originate from the vibration of disordered (D) graphite band in the termination plane and graphitic (G) band of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional lattice, respectively. The 2D peak at about 2754 cm−1 is strongly suppressed. The ID/IG of N-GQDs is calculated to be about 1.78, indicating the presence of edges and isolated defects in N-GQDs [26]. The corresponding atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 2(e)) reveals the topographic morphology of evenly dispersed N-GQDs after spin coating. Fig. 2(f) and (g) show the corresponding thicknesses of N-GQDs in Fig. 2(e). The thicknesses of N-GQDs are primarily between 0.5 and 1.5 nm. Fig. 2(h) shows the height distribution histogram of N-GQDs, and the average height is about 0.8 nm, which indicates that most of the N-GQDs consist of 1–3 layers. Furthermore, the heights of each point were analyzed into statistic data in Fig. S2.

The surface compositions and the chemical bonds of the synthesized N-GQDs were measured by XPS. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3(a)) of the N-GQDs shows three dominate peaks of C-1s at 285.6 eV, N-1s at 400.59 eV, and O-1s at 532.29 eV, respectively. Fig. S3 shows that the corresponding atomic ratio of C/O/N was around 61/37/2. This result confirms the successful doping of N in N-GQDs. The C/O atomic ratio of N-GQDs indicates a good contribution from carbonyl and carbon hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs. The high-resolution C-1s spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) reveals the presence of CC/CC (285.1 eV), CN (285.6 eV), COH/CNH2 (286.2 eV), and CO (287.2 eV) functional groups. Moreover, the high-resolution N-1s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) demonstrates the presence of two relative nitrogen species of –NH2 (399.6 eV) and pyrrolic N (401.4 eV) with a ratio of 0.34:0.76. The O-1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) further confirms these observations and two typical oxygen-containing chemical bond of CO (532.4 eV) and COH/COC (533.2 eV) exist [27–29]. As shown in Fig. S4, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) spectrum of dopamine provides a reference for the reaction temperature, and that of N-GQDs reveals an initial ~10% weight loss up to ~180 °C in nitrogen, corresponding to the removal of oxygen containing groups due to the partial reduction of N-GQDs [30]. As illustrated in Fig. 3(e), the FT-IR spectrum exhibits characteristic absorption bands of OH and CH stretching vibrations at 3424 and 2932 cm−1. The wide and strong peak at 1450–1650 cm−1 is attributed to the skeletal vibrations of the aromatic rings. The stretching vibrations of CO at 1673 cm−1 and CO at 1196 cm−1 confirm the carbonyl groups on the surface, enabling the N-GQDs to possess good water-solubility [23,31]. In addition, the peaks at 1384 and 3250 cm−1 could be ascribed to –NH and CNHC bands, which is consistent with the results of XPS, indicating the existence of nitrogen-containing internal structure and edge functional groups. To verify the yield enhancement effect of the surface-rich functional groups, quinine sulfate was chosen as a reference, the QY of the N-GQDs in this work was calculated to be 0.34 as shown in Fig. S5, which is higher than those of GQDs (include reported N-GQDs) [22,32–36], as summarized in Fig. 3(f).

The most significant feature of the N-GQDs that reported in our work relies on their fluorescence property. Fig. 4(a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescent emission spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solutions. Typical π-π⁎ and n-π⁎ transitions centering at ~220 and ~280 nm are observed, corresponding to the transition model of aromatic sp2 domains [11] and oxygen-containing groups [20], respectively. From the PL spectrum, a strong emission with the maximum emission wavelength of 562 nm could be observed under a 420 nm light excitation. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of PL is approximately 100 nm, which was similar to that of the most present GQDs [37,38]. The N-GQDs aqueous solution is pale yellow under the sunlight (Fig. 4(b)), while the bright yellow luminescence can be observed under 365 nm UV light (as shown in Fig. 4(c)). This strong fluorescence gives the promise applications in fluorescence imaging. Furthermore, the prepared N-GQDs show emission wavelength which is independence on the excitation wavelength when it was changed from 360 to 480 nm (Fig. 4(d)). The excitation-independent behavior of the N-GQDs was attributed to the uniform size and the surface state of the sp2 clusters [39]. The fluorescent stability of the N-GQDs is further investigated under different conditions. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs exhibits negligible change after being irradiated under 365 nm UV light radiation for 120 min and incubated for two weeks (Fig. 4(e) and (f)), showing their excellent photostability. The influence of pH value on the fluorescence performance of N-GQDs is also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4(g), as increasing of the pH value, the PL intensities rapidly increases and arrives to a maximum value at pH = 7.0 and then decrease. All of these results indicate the N-GQDs are valuable for biological applications.

The high QY and stable luminescence illustrate N-GQD is an ideal candidate in fluorescence bioimaging. Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs with the present of cations (20 μM), including Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Au3+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, K+ and Na+, has been shown in Fig. 5(a). Only Fe3+ ions caused an extreme quenching, it indicates the N-GQDs has a satisfactory selectivity and can be applied for detecting of Fe3+. It also demonstrates N-GQDs based Fe3+ detector has the excellent antijamming capability owning to the adamant lattice doping structure in N-GQDs [40]. Fig. 5(b) shows the PL stability of N-GQDs even when the concentration of KCl higher than 1 M.

If N-GQD is in biological application, the toxicity is crucial and must be demonstrated carefully. Here, viabilities of fibroblast cells are placed in the N-GQDs solutions of different concentrations for 24 h at 37 °C (Fig. 5(c)). No evidence shows the reduction of cell viability even the concentration of N-GQDs is up to 300 μg/mL. It indicates the N-GQDs produced by our method are low cytotoxicity and show excellent biocompatibility in vitro. Furthermore, the yellow PL emitted inside the cell suggests that the N-GQDs performed as an efficient bioimaging material for cell detecting (Fig. 5(d)).

In order to obtain the best performance for Fe3+ assay, the reaction time is optimized. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs is acutely quenched within 1 min upon the addition of Fe3+ and then keeping stable. These results provide the possibility for rapid and stable detection of Fe3+. Fig. 6(a) shows the change of fluorescence intensity (at 562 nm) of N-GQDs solutions after adding Fe3+ of different concentrates in the range of 0–300 μM. The fluorescence intensity quenching of N-GQDs increases gradually with the increase of Fe3+. The change of color is detected by the naked eye under both daylight and UV light (Fig. 6(b)). Specifically, Fig. 6(c) presents the change in fluorescence intensity, defined as (F0 − F) / F0 (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs at 562 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively), as a function of Fe3+ concentration. It should be noted that the change in fluorescence intensity increases rapidly in the low Fe3+ concentration range and then increases slowly to a plateau when Fe3+ concentration increases to 200 μM. A good linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) is observed over the concentration ranging from 0 to 80 μM and the detection limit of 63 nM is also obtained (Fig. 6(d)), which is relatively lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency allowed maximum tolerance (5.4 μM) in drinking water and most of the recently reported assays for Fe3+ detection in literatures [41–48]. Table 1 shows the comparison of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection, suggesting our sensing system exhibits a superior sensitivity.

The quenching mechanism process is studied to catch the reasons of its high sensitivity and selectivity. It has been reported that catechol group of dopamine can coordinate with Fe3+ ions and then oxidize the related quinone molecule [49]. This oxidation process has been already established as a known potent electron acceptor in biological and abiotic formats [50,51]. Herein, a different reaction mechanism based on the complexation and electron transfer between Fe3+ and nitrogen doping of N-GQDs (–NH2 and CNC) has been proposed. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the fluorescence quenching in solution is fitted by a typical Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + KSV[Fe3+], where F0 and F are the PL intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The KSV is the static Stern-Volmer quenching constant. In view of the fact that both dynamic quenching effect and static quenching effect fit well with Stern-Volmer equation, thus, they could not be simply differentiated using this equation. To better understand the Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism, we investigate the PL decay of N-GQDs which is measured by a time-correlated single-photon counting technique and fitted by a bi-exponential function, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The average PL lifetime of N-GQDs is calculated to be 2.56 ns. After adding 10 and 20 μM Fe3+, the corresponding lifetime is found to be around 2.54 and 2.55 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs exhibits negligible change before and after adding Fe3+, indicating no significant excited-state interaction between N-GQDs and Fe3+ [52]. This observation demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching provoked by Fe3+ is obedient to the static quenching effect that arises from the formation of a stable non-fluorescence complex between surface nitrogen functional groups of N-GQDs and Fe3+ in the ground state. To prove the reliability of the explanation, UV–vis absorption spectra and zeta potential are performed. The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs with various concentrations of Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 7(c). The absorption peak of the N-GQDs slightly shifted to a shorter wavelength (from 403 to 357 nm) after adding Fe3+. In contrast to adding other metal ions in N-GQDs (Fig. S7) that without any detectable shift, the shift induced by addition of Fe3+ can be attributed to the formation of some complexes between N-GQDs and Fe3+. Fig. 7(d) exhibits the zeta potentials of N-GQDs after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ into the system. Noticeably, the zeta potentials of N-GQDs decrease gradually from −49.8 to −34.7 mV as the Fe3+ concentration increases. It has been reported that the zeta potential becomes more positive if most Fe3+ are coordinated with the carboxyl or hydroxyl group of N-GQDs [53,54]. Therefore, it is deduced that Fe3+ could coordinate with multiple nitrogen-containing functional groups. Meanwhile, carboxyls or hydroxyls may also coordinate with Fe3+ [47], revealing a firm combination between Fe3+ and N-GQDs via strong electrostatic interactions. Based on all above results, one can conclude that the complexation and electron transfer between Fe3+ and nitrogen doping of N-GQDs (–NH2 and CNC) result in effective quenching and the decrease in fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs solution.

Finally, the practical feasibility of the assay was further investigated by detecting Fe3+ in water solution. The recovery experiments are performed by measuring fluorescence responses to different water samples, in which several Fe3+ with different concentrations are spiked into the sample. The concentration of Fe3+ is determined from the calibration curves. The results of Fe3+ detection in water solution are shown in Table 2 . Obviously, the results of the two samples are satisfied, demonstrating that the applicability of the proposed methodology.

In summary, we have synthesized highly photoluminescent N-GQDs with bright yellow emission via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment of dopamine. Remarkably, the N-GQDs exhibit a high fluorescence quantum yield of about 34%, favorable biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and strong resistance to photobleaching. The N-GQDs can be used as a promising fluorescent probe for cellular imaging. It also shows a high sensitivity and selectivity in terms of detecting Fe3+. A linear change of fluorescence intensity with the Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 80 μM with a detection limit of 63 nM is demonstrated in our work. Thus, it can be utilized to detect Fe3+ in natural water. The results suggest the N-GQDs synthesized here are of great values in designing effective fluorescence probes and detecting metal ions in environmental application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084 and 51602056), K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: XRD spectrum, height statistic data, and atomic ratio of N-GQDs. TGA curves of DA and N-GQDs. The linear relationship between fluorescence integrated intensity and corresponding optical density from absorbance of N-GQDs and Quinine sulfate. Effect of reaction time of N-GQDs and Fe3+. UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs with the present of different metal ions. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2020.107749.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots: the role of many-body effects
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Y. H.; Shu, H. B.; Wang, S. D.; Wang, J. L. Electronic and Optical Properties of Graphene Quantum Dots: The Role of Many-Body Effects. J. Phys. Chem. C 2015, 119, 4983-4989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent TPA@GQDs probe for sensitive assay and quantitative imaging of hydroxyl radicals in living cells
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hai, X.; Guo, Z. Y.; Lin, X.; Chen, X. W.; Wang, J. H. Fluorescent TPA@GQDs Probe for Sensitive Assay and Quantitative Imaging of Hydroxyl Radicals in Living Cells. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2018, 10, 5853-5861.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
